# ICJIA File Accessibility Audit — Full Documentation

> Comprehensive reference for LLMs, AI agents, and automated tools.

## Overview

The ICJIA File Accessibility Audit is a web application that analyzes PDF (.pdf), Word (.docx), PowerPoint (.pptx), and Excel (.xlsx) documents for accessibility. The file format is detected from file content (magic bytes / package structure), never from the filename extension or a declared MIME type.

Each document is scored under a single methodology: a weighted, partial-credit 0-100 score (A-F grade) across the WCAG-aligned categories that apply to its format, plus a separate binary WCAG 2.2 Level AA conformance verdict computed independently of the weighted score. The score shows how close a document is and what to fix first; the conformance verdict is the honest pass/fail answer, because WCAG conformance is all-or-nothing per success criterion (one image missing alt text fails 1.1.1 outright) and a partial-credit score cannot itself be a conformance claim. Neither is a legal compliance determination — pair the audit with a human accessibility review (e.g., PAC 2024, Adobe Acrobat's Accessibility Full Check, or each Office app's built-in Accessibility Checker) for a definitive determination.

PDF analysis uses two complementary parsers: QPDF (a subprocess) for the tag/structure tree, and pdfjs-dist (in-process) for text and metadata extraction. Word, PowerPoint, and Excel analysis parses the OOXML package (a ZIP of XML parts) directly in JavaScript with JSZip and fast-xml-parser — no external binary — inside a dedicated, interruptible child process, so a pathological Office file can be killed outright on timeout rather than merely abandoned. All four formats share the same concurrency semaphore (2 simultaneous analyses); a request beyond that limit queues or returns HTTP 503. No file content is retained — analysis is in-memory only.

PDFs can optionally be auto-remediated (tag structure added via QPDF + OpenDataLoader, with an optional veraPDF PDF/UA-1 conformance check) — this is a separate, PDF-only feature, disabled by default (`REMEDIATION_ENABLED=true` to turn it on), with its own audit-before-remediate gate and strict file-retention limits (output deleted on first download or a 30-minute TTL). Word, PowerPoint, and Excel are audit-only; there is no auto-remediation path for those formats.

Website: https://audit.icjia.app
Source: https://github.com/ICJIA/file-accessibility-audit
FAQs: https://accessibility.icjia.app
Organization: Illinois Criminal Justice Information Authority (https://icjia.illinois.gov)

---

## Scoring Model

Every audit produces exactly one score. A prior release (before v1.21.0) surfaced two named score profiles ("Strict" and "Practical/PDF-UA") side by side; that dual-profile system was retired because it confused auditors, and PDF/UA-1 conformance is now verified more directly by the optional veraPDF check on the remediation result page. There is no user-facing choice of scoring methodology today — every report shows one 0-100 score and grade.

Within that single score:

- Categories that don't apply to a given document (no tables, no images, no links, etc.) are excluded from the weighted average and the remaining categories' weights are renormalized, so a document is never penalized for lacking content it was never going to have.
- A category can be null for one of two distinct reasons, both rendered as "N/A" in the score math but labeled differently in the report: **"Not Applicable"** means the document genuinely has none of that content (e.g., no tables to check); **"Not Assessed"** means the tool does not (or in some cases cannot yet) automatically evaluate that category for that format (e.g., PDF color contrast; Word reading order). See Category Details below for exactly which categories fall into "Not Assessed" per format.
- The separate WCAG 2.2 conformance verdict has three possible states: confirmed automated failures (each tied to a specific WCAG success criterion and linked to its W3C "Understanding" page), "no automated WCAG failures detected" (never phrased as "conformant," since contrast and the semantic *correctness* of alt text/headings/reading order/tags require human judgment), or "incomplete" (the analyzer could not process the file — e.g., it's encrypted or corrupt — so no verdict could be determined).

---

## Category Details

Category weights by format (a scored category's weights within one format always sum to 100%; "not assessed" categories carry no weight and are excluded from the average):

| Category | PDF | Word | PowerPoint | Excel | WCAG SC (Level) |
| --- | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | --- |
| Text Extractability | 20% | 5% | 5% | 5% | 1.1.1, 1.3.1 (A) |
| Title & Language | 15% | 18% | 14% | 12%\* | 2.4.2, 3.1.1 (A) |
| Heading Structure | 15% | 18% | — | — | 1.3.1 (A), 2.4.6 (AA) |
| Slide Titles | — | — | 18% | — | 1.3.1 (A), 2.4.6 (AA) |
| Sheet Names | — | — | — | 18% | 2.4.6 (AA) |
| Alt Text on Images | 15% | 18% | 18% | 18% | 1.1.1 (A) |
| Table Markup | 10% | 12% | 10% | 25% | 1.3.1 (A) |
| Reading Order | 10% | not assessed | 10% | — | 1.3.2 (A) |
| Bookmarks / Navigation | 5% | — | — | — | 2.4.5 (AA) |
| Color Contrast | not assessed | 12% | 10% | 12% | 1.4.3 (AA) |
| List Structure | — | 9% | 7% | — | 1.3.1 (A) |
| Link & URL Quality | 5% | 8% | 8% | 10% | 2.4.4 (A) |
| Form Accessibility | 5% | not assessed | not assessed | not assessed | 1.3.1, 3.3.2, 4.1.2 (A) |

"—" = this category does not exist for that format's report at all. "not assessed" = the category card is shown but always carries a null score with an explanatory note. \*Excel has no document-language property, so the 3.1.1 half of Title & Language is always "not assessed" for Excel and the category scores purely on title presence (100 if a workbook title is set, 0 if not) — PDF/Word/PowerPoint split the 100 points 50/50 between title and language.

### Text Extractability
PDF 20% · Word 5% · PowerPoint 5% · Excel 5%. WCAG 1.1.1, 1.3.1 (A).
For PDF, this checks whether the document has real, selectable text and a tag structure (StructTreeRoot) — the most fundamental accessibility requirement, since a scanned image with no real text gives a screen reader nothing to read at all (a fully scanned, untagged PDF scores 0 here). Word, PowerPoint, and Excel always store real text in their XML (never a flat image), so this check auto-passes at 100 for those three formats; it is kept at a small weight there specifically so a structureless Office document can't coast to a high score on this alone.

### Title & Language
PDF 15% · Word 18% · PowerPoint 14% · Excel 12%. WCAG 2.4.2 Page Titled (A), 3.1.1 Language of Page (A).
Checks the document's metadata for a title (the first thing a screen reader announces) and a declared language (which controls pronunciation rules). PDF, Word, and PowerPoint split the score 50/50 between the two. Excel workbooks have no document-language property, so this category scores on title presence alone (100 or 0) and WCAG 3.1.1 is always listed as "not assessed" for Excel specifically.

### Heading Structure
PDF 15% · Word 18%. WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A), 2.4.6 Headings and Labels (AA).
Checks for a real, logically-ordered heading hierarchy — H1-H6 structure tags in PDF, "Heading 1"-"Heading 6" paragraph styles in Word — flagging skipped levels and, for PDF, generic unleveled heading tags. Word additionally flags "fake headings": text visually formatted to look like a heading (bold, larger font) without a real Heading style applied, which is invisible to assistive technology even though it looks like a heading on screen. Not applicable to PowerPoint (which has Slide Titles instead, PowerPoint's outline mechanism) or Excel.

### Slide Titles
PowerPoint 18% only — PowerPoint-specific; there is no separate Heading Structure category for slides. WCAG 1.3.1 (A), 2.4.6 (AA).
Every slide needs a title placeholder — PowerPoint's equivalent of a heading — so screen-reader users can tell slides apart in the outline and jump directly to one. Flags slides with no title and slides that duplicate another slide's title.

### Sheet Names
Excel 18% only — Excel-specific. WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels (AA).
Checks that visible worksheets have descriptive names rather than Excel's defaults ("Sheet1", "Sheet2", ...). Sheet names are a workbook's primary navigation mechanism, announced by screen readers when switching between sheets.

### Alt Text on Images
PDF 15% · Word 18% · PowerPoint 18% · Excel 18%. WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A).
The same check across all four formats: the percentage of non-decorative images, charts, and graphics that have alternative text. Images explicitly marked decorative are excluded from the count (a document where every image is decorative scores 100). Not applicable if the document has no images.

### Table Markup
PDF 10% · Word 12% · PowerPoint 10% · Excel 25%. WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A).
Checks that data tables have a marked header row/cell so screen readers can associate each cell with its row/column header as a user navigates. Word and PowerPoint also flag nested tables as hard to navigate. Excel weights this category highest of any format (25%) — whether tabular data is a real Excel Table object with a header row, versus a plain unstructured range of typed cells, is the single biggest accessibility factor for a spreadsheet; Excel also flags merged cells as an advisory finding (not a hard penalty), since they can confuse screen-reader cell navigation.

### Reading Order
PDF 10% (active) · PowerPoint 10% (active) · Word: always "not assessed" · not applicable to Excel. WCAG 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence (A).
For PDF, this checks structure-tree depth and content ordering (MCID sequence) against the page's content stream. For PowerPoint, it checks that each slide's title is the first shape in that slide's shape/tab order, so a screen reader announces the title before the body content — dense slides (10+ shapes) get an additional advisory to check manually. For Word, the linear document flow generally preserves reading order automatically, and this tool does not evaluate floating objects, text boxes, or wrapped images, so the category is always shown as "not assessed" rather than scored (this is the one category where PowerPoint is scored but Word — otherwise the more fully-scored Office format — is not).

### Bookmarks / Navigation
PDF 5% only — not present at all in the Word, PowerPoint, or Excel category sets. WCAG 2.4.5 Multiple Ways (AA).
Checks for outline/bookmark entries, but only for PDFs of 10 or more pages; shorter documents score "not applicable" rather than being penalized for missing bookmarks.

### Color Contrast
PDF: always "not assessed" (rendered-PDF contrast analysis is not yet implemented — this is the one category never computed for PDF at all). Word 12% · PowerPoint 10% · Excel 12%. WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) (AA).
Word, PowerPoint, and Excel all store explicit colors in their XML (text-run colors, shape fills, cell styles), so contrast is computed directly wherever a document specifies an explicit foreground and background color, checked against the WCAG minimums (4.5:1 normal text, 3:1 large text). Colors inherited from a theme or style default are not resolved in this version and are flagged as an advisory for manual review rather than scored — a document where every checked color is theme-inherited (nothing explicit to check) shows this category as "not assessed" rather than scored.

### List Structure
Word 9% · PowerPoint 7% — not present for PDF or Excel. WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A).
Distinguishes real list formatting (Word's Bullets/Numbering styles; PowerPoint's bullet/numbering formatting), which lets a screen reader announce "list, N items" and each item's position, from manually-typed bullet characters ("-", "*", etc.), which are read aloud as plain text with no list semantics.

### Link & URL Quality
PDF 5% · Word 8% · PowerPoint 8% · Excel 10%. WCAG 2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context) (A).
The percentage of hyperlinks that use descriptive text rather than a raw URL or a vague phrase ("click here", "read more", "this link") — link text that's meaningless when a screen-reader user pulls up a list of links out of context. Not applicable if the document has no links.

### Form Accessibility
PDF 5% (scored) · always "not assessed" for Word, PowerPoint, and Excel. WCAG 1.3.1 (A), 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions (A), 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A).
For PDF, this is the percentage of interactive form fields that have a tooltip label (the `/TU` attribute). Interactive form controls are uncommon in Word, PowerPoint, and Excel files and are not automatically assessed in those three formats in this version.

### PDF-only signals (not part of the score)
Two additional PDF-specific outputs are attached to a PDF report but are informational — they never affect the weighted score or the categories above, and neither is present at all for Word/PowerPoint/Excel reports:
- **PDF/UA-1 signals** — a "conformance signals" panel summarizing machine-checkable PDF/UA (ISO 14289-1) indicators: whether a PDF/UA identifier is declared, structure-tree presence and depth, `/MarkInfo`/`Marked` status, artifact-content run counts, and font-embedding completeness. This is a signals summary, not a formal PDF/UA conformance verdict (a full PDF/UA-1 validation, including the Matterhorn Protocol's failure conditions, needs a dedicated tool like PAC 2024 or veraPDF — the latter is also what the optional auto-remediation feature runs).
- **Adobe parity report** — mirrors Adobe Acrobat's built-in Accessibility Checker (32 binary rules) alongside this tool's own verdict, so results can be reconciled with what a reviewer sees in Acrobat. Qualitative only, not an aggregated score.

---

## Grade Scale

| Grade | Score Range | Label |
| :---: | :---: | --- |
| A | 90-100 | Excellent |
| B | 80-89 | Good |
| C | 70-79 | Needs Improvement |
| D | 60-69 | Poor |
| F | 0-59 | Failing |

## Severity Levels

Each category also carries an individual severity label, derived from that category's own score (distinct from the overall letter grade above):

| Severity | Score | Meaning |
| --- | :--: | --- |
| No issues found | 100 | The automated checks for this category found nothing — reserved for a perfect 100; a 90-99 still has at least one finding. |
| Minor | 70-99 | Small improvements recommended. |
| Moderate | 40-69 | Should be addressed before publishing. |
| Critical | 0-39 | A significant barrier to access — must be fixed. |

## Standards Alignment

- **WCAG 2.2 Level AA** — the default, operative standard (a strict superset of WCAG 2.1 AA; every machine-checkable criterion carried forward unchanged from 2.1 into 2.2, so automated results are identical either way). WCAG 2.2 adds six new Level A/AA success criteria beyond 2.1, all interactive/manual (e.g., target size, dragging movements, redundant entry) — none are automatically failed by this tool; where one could plausibly apply (mainly to interactive PDF form fields) it is surfaced as "not assessed — manual review," never as an automated failure. A deployment can revert its displayed standard to WCAG 2.1 via the `WCAG_VERSION=2.1` environment variable.
- **ADA Title II** — the U.S. Department of Justice's digital accessibility rule for state and local government, requiring WCAG 2.1 AA; effective April 2026.
- **Illinois IITAA 2.1** (§E205.4) — the Illinois Information Technology Accessibility Act; frames final non-web document accessibility through WCAG 2.1 AA, the legal minimum this tool's default WCAG 2.2 auditing exceeds.
- **Section 508** — the U.S. federal accessibility standard, aligned with WCAG 2.0/2.1 AA.
- **PDF/UA** (ISO 14289-1) — referenced only for PDF (see "PDF-only signals" above); not applicable to Word, PowerPoint, or Excel reports.

---

## JSON Export Schema (v2.1)

This is the schema of the downloadable **JSON export** (the "Export as JSON" action on a report) — a richer, wrapped shape built for LLM/automation consumption. It is distinct from the raw `POST /api/analyze` API response; see API Reference below for that flatter shape.

```json
{
  "reportMeta": {
    "generatedAt": "ISO 8601 timestamp",
    "generatedAtFormatted": "human-readable date",
    "tool": "ICJIA File Accessibility Audit",
    "toolUrl": "https://audit.icjia.app",
    "schemaVersion": "2.1"
  },
  "file": {
    "name": "filename.pdf",
    "pages": 12,
    "isScanned": false
  },
  "score": {
    "overall": 85,
    "grade": "B",
    "gradeLabel": "Good",
    "primaryMode": "strict"
  },
  "scoreProfiles": {
    "strict": {
      "label": "Strict semantic score (WCAG + IITAA §E205.4)",
      "description": "...",
      "overall": 85,
      "grade": "B",
      "gradeLabel": "Good",
      "executiveSummary": "..."
    }
  },
  "executiveSummary": "Human-readable summary of findings",
  "warnings": [],
  "conformance": {
    "status": "fail | no-automated-failures | incomplete",
    "failures": [
      { "sc": "1.1.1", "name": "Non-text Content", "level": "A", "category": "Alt Text on Images", "issue": "...", "url": "..." }
    ],
    "notAssessed": [
      { "sc": "1.4.3", "name": "Contrast (Minimum)", "level": "AA", "reason": "...", "url": "..." }
    ],
    "headline": "Does not meet WCAG 2.2 Level AA | No automated WCAG failures detected | WCAG verdict could not be determined"
  },
  "categories": [
    {
      "id": "text_extractability",
      "label": "Text Extractability",
      "score": 100,
      "grade": "A",
      "severity": "No issues found",
      "status": "pass | minor | moderate | fail | not-applicable",
      "findings": ["Human-readable finding strings"],
      "explanation": "What this category checks",
      "helpLinks": [{ "label": "...", "url": "..." }],
      "wcag": {
        "successCriteria": ["1.3.1 Info and Relationships (Level A)"],
        "successCriteriaDetailed": [{ "sc": "1.3.1", "name": "Info and Relationships", "level": "A", "url": "..." }],
        "principle": "Perceivable",
        "remediation": "Step-by-step fix instructions"
      }
    }
  ],
  "remediationPlan": {
    "summary": "N categories need remediation.",
    "prioritizedSteps": [
      {
        "priority": 1,
        "category": "Category Name",
        "currentScore": 0,
        "severity": "Critical",
        "wcagCriteria": ["1.3.1 Info and Relationships (Level A)"],
        "action": "Specific remediation steps"
      }
    ]
  },
  "llmContext": {
    "description": "Structured context for LLMs",
    "prompt": "Pre-built prompt summarizing the audit",
    "standards": [
      "WCAG 2.2 Level AA",
      "ADA Title II (effective April 2026)",
      "Illinois IITAA 2.1 (§E205.4)",
      "Section 508",
      "PDF/UA (ISO 14289-1)"
    ],
    "scoringScale": {
      "pass": "90–100",
      "minor": "70–89",
      "moderate": "40–69",
      "fail": "0–39"
    }
  }
}
```

Notes on this schema:
- `file.pages` is always the numeric page/slide/sheet count, whatever the format calls it (PDF and Word: pages; PowerPoint: slides; Excel: sheets).
- `score` is the single canonical score. `scoreProfiles` is always a single-entry object keyed `"strict"` — a structural remnant of a retired multi-profile schema, kept only so older integrations built against it keep parsing; there is no second entry and none is planned. New integrations should read `score` directly.
- `categories[].status` uses 90/70/40 boundaries (distinct from, and slightly different at the top end than, the category `severity` label's 100/70/40 boundaries described under Severity Levels above); `"not-applicable"` covers both the "Not Applicable" and "Not Assessed" cases described in Category Details — the underlying distinction is visible in the category's `findings` text but is not currently its own JSON field.
- `"PDF/UA (ISO 14289-1)"` only appears in `llmContext.standards` for PDF reports — it is omitted for Word/PowerPoint/Excel exports.

---

## API Reference

Endpoints marked "Auth: session or PAT" accept either a browser session cookie or an `Authorization: Bearer fap_xxx` personal access token. The public deployment currently runs with authentication disabled (anonymous mode), so in practice no credential is required there; a deployment with `AUTH.REQUIRE_LOGIN=true` enforces it.

### POST /api/analyze
Upload a document for accessibility analysis.
- Content-Type: multipart/form-data
- Field: `file` — PDF, Word (.docx), PowerPoint (.pptx), or Excel (.xlsx), max 15 MB. The format is detected from file content, not the filename or declared MIME type.
- Auth: session or PAT (not enforced in anonymous-mode deployments)
- Returns the raw analysis result — a flatter object than the JSON Export Schema above: `{ filename, pageCount, fileType, pdfMetadata|docxMetadata|pptxMetadata|xlsxMetadata, overallScore, grade, isScanned, executiveSummary, categories, warnings, scoringMode, scoreProfiles, conformance, pdfUa?, adobeParity? }`. `pdfUa` and `adobeParity` are present only for PDF. `scoreProfiles` here is the same single-entry (`strict`) object described above.
- Errors: 400 no file / unsupported file type; 415 format disabled on this server; 422 corrupt or unparseable file, or password-protected PDF; 503 server busy (concurrency limit reached); 504 analysis timed out.

### POST /api/analyze-url
Audit a document by URL instead of upload. Returns the same result shape as `POST /api/analyze` (not persisted — no report link is created).
- Content-Type: application/json
- Body: `{ "url": "https://..." }`
- Auth: session or PAT
- The URL must resolve to a public host. Anonymous callers are restricted to an ICJIA / Illinois state government allowlist (extendable via the `ANALYZE_URL_ALLOWED_HOSTS` environment variable); a request bearing a valid privileged bearer token bypasses the allowlist and may fetch any public URL. Either way, a private/reserved-IP SSRF block (localhost, `*.local`/`*.internal`, RFC1918 ranges, link-local addresses) is enforced unconditionally, re-checked on every redirect hop.
- Limits: 15 MB fetched content, 30 s fetch timeout, same rate limit as `/api/analyze`.

### POST /api/audit-url
Audit a document by URL **and** persist a shareable report in one call — built for fleet-inventory automation that needs both a score and a stable link to the full report, in a response shape that flattens directly into a CSV row.
- Content-Type: application/json
- Body: `{ "url": "https://...", "force"?: boolean }` — accepts PDF, Word, PowerPoint, or Excel, detected from the fetched content.
- Auth: session or PAT
- Hash dedup: after fetching, the server hashes the content; if an unexpired report already exists for that hash and caller, the cached report is returned (`"cached": true`) and no new audit runs. Pass `"force": true` (or `?force=true`) to bypass and force a fresh audit.
- Returns: `{ filename, pageCount, audited, strict: { score, grade }, practical: { score, grade }, reportId, reportUrl, reportExpiresAt, cached }`. `strict` and `practical` always carry identical values — `practical` is a deprecated backward-compatibility alias of `strict`, kept only for fleet-CSV consumers built before the scoring system was unified into one score; either key can be read safely.
- Shared reports created here expire after 365 days.

### POST /api/bulk-from-inventory
Accepts a [filecap](https://github.com/ICJIA/filecap-cli) NDJSON inventory and scores every file in it (any of the four formats, dispatched by detected content type) server-side in one request.
- Content-Type: `text/plain` (raw NDJSON, max 5 MB) or `application/json` (`{ "inventory": "<NDJSON string>" }`)
- Auth: session or PAT
- Limits: 100 files per request (additional entries are silently skipped), 15 MB per file, 30 s fetch timeout per file — files are processed serially through the same 2-at-a-time concurrency semaphore shared with every other audit endpoint, so large inventories can take several minutes in one HTTP request.
- Returns: `{ "summary": { "total": n, "analyzed": n, "failed": n, "skipped": n }, "results": [ { "sha256", "path", "publicUrl", "overallScore", "grade", "reportId", "reportUrl", "error" } ] }` — one result entry per inventory line, `error` present instead of the score fields when that file failed.

### POST /api/reports
Create a shareable report link.
- Content-Type: application/json
- Body: `{ "report": <analysis result object> }`
- Auth: session or PAT
- Returns: `{ "id": "uuid", "expiresAt": "ISO date" }`

### GET /api/reports/:id
Retrieve a shared report. Public — no authentication.
- Returns: `{ "report": <analysis result>, "createdAt": "ISO date", "expiresAt": "ISO date" }`
- 404 if not found; 410 if the 365-day link has expired.

### GET /api/health
Health check.
- Returns: `{ "status": "ok", "uptime": "2h 15m 30s" }` (a human-formatted duration string, not a raw number).

### Command-line interface
The monorepo also ships `a11y-audit`, a CLI that runs the identical scoring engine against local files — `a11y-audit report.pdf`, `a11y-audit slides.pptx --json`, or `a11y-audit docs/*.docx --threshold 80` for a CI gate that exits 1 if any file scores below the threshold. A separate `publist` batch mode audits an entire publication catalog and generates CSV/HTML summary reports. See the source repository for build and usage instructions.
